perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with principles of equity and fairness as determined by the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to New York law and replacing it with a vague standard allows the Discloser to potentially avoid specific legal obligations under New York law, which protects the Recipient. This increases legal risk for the Recipient as the Discloser's interpretation may be biased or inconsistent with established legal principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-326 prohibits agreements that exempt lessors from liability for negligence. By removing the governing law clause, and replacing it with vague standard of fairness, the contract opens door to the Discloser being able to impose unfair obligations on the Recipient that might be considered against public policy or be deemed to be unconscionable, which is something that the clause was protecting against with its link to New York law. This clause aims to prevent parties with superior bargaining power from using contracts to avoid responsibility for their own negligence.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-326 Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence... General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-326. Agreements exempting pools, gymnasiums, places of public amusement or recreation and similar establishments from liability for negligence void and unenforceable. Every covenant, agreement or understanding in or in connection with, or collateral to, any contract, membership application, ticket of admission or similar writing, entered into between the owner or operator of any pool, gymnasium, place of amusement or recreation, or similar establishment and the user of such facilities, pursuant to which such owner or operator receives a fee or other compensation for the use of such facilities, which exempts the said owner or operator from liability for damages caused by or resulting from the negligence of the owner, operator or person in charge of such establishment, or their agents, servants or employees, shall be deemed to be void as against public policy and wholly unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures the agreement is governed by New York law. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of equity and fairness as determined by the Discloser,' removing the legal certainty and consumer protection provided by New York law. NY GOL § 5-326 prohibits agreements that exempt lessors from liability for negligence, and replacing the governing law with a vague standard opens the door for the Discloser to impose unfair obligations on the Recipient. The change contradicts the legal intent to protect the Recipient, by removing the shield of NY law.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor or generated by the Recipient pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will return to the Disclosor, upon request, any documents or other tangible materials disclosed by the Disclosor pertaining to the Discussion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or generated by the Recipient' could allow the Discloser to claim ownership of intellectual property that the Recipient independently developed using their own resources, even if it was inspired by the disclosed information. This contradicts general principles of intellectual property law that protect independently created works.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 102 protects original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression. By requiring the return of materials *generated* by the recipient, the altered clause implicitly challenges the recipient's potential copyright ownership over derivative works or independently created works that may incorporate or be inspired by the confidential information. This removal introduces ambiguity and potential for IP disputes, contradicting the principles of copyright law that protect independently created works, even if related to disclosed information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works ; (2) musical works, including any accompanying words; (3) dramatic works, including any accompanying music; (4) pantomimes and choreographic works; (5) pictorial, graphic, and sculptural works; (6) motion pictures and other audiovisual works ; (7) sound recordings ; and (8) architectural works . (b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process , system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied in such work. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2544 ; Pub. L. 101–650, title VII, § 703 , Dec. 1, 1990 , 104 Stat. 5133 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Original Works of Authorship. The two fundamental criteria of copyright protection—originality and fixation in tangible form are restated in the first sentence of this cornerstone provision. The phrase “original works or authorship,” which is purposely left undefined, is intended to incorporate without change the standard of originality established by the courts under the present copyright statute. This standard does not include requirements of novelty, ingenuity, or esthetic merit, and there is no intention to enlarge the standard of copyright protection to require them

Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works; (2) musical works, including
perturbation.justification: The original contract required the Recipient to return documents disclosed by the Discloser or generated by the Recipient. The modified contract removes the phrase 'or generated by the Recipient'. According to the Law Explanation, this challenges the recipient's copyright ownership over derivative or independently created works, which contradicts the principles of copyright law. 17 U.S.C. § 102, as shown in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2, protects original works of authorship. By removing the obligation to return materials 'generated by the Recipient', the modified contract creates a scenario where the Discloser could potentially claim ownership over the Recipient's independently created works, thereby undermining the Recipient's copyright as protected under 17 U.S.C. § 102. The omission directly impacts the Recipient's rights to their own work, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information who are bound in writing to keep it confidential and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will disclose Confidential Information only to its employees, agents and representatives with a need to know such Confidential Information and only to the extent necessary for the Discussion to be conducted.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'who are bound in writing to keep it confidential' creates a loophole where the Recipient can disclose confidential information to employees, agents, and representatives without ensuring they are legally obligated to maintain confidentiality. This significantly increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure and potential misuse of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S.C. § 1836, aims to protect trade secrets. By removing the requirement that the Recipient's employees/agents/representatives be 'bound in writing to keep it confidential', the modified clause weakens the reasonable measures taken to protect the confidentiality of the information, potentially undermining the Discloser's ability to claim trade secret protection under the DTSA should a leak occur. The written agreement provides clear evidence of intent to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The omission of 'who are bound in writing to keep it confidential' directly undermines the protection of confidential information. While scraped law snippet 2 is a 404 error, scraped law snippet 1 discusses civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation under 18 U.S. Code § 1836. The law explanation states that requiring employees/agents/representatives to be bound in writing demonstrates 'reasonable measures taken to protect the confidentiality of the information.' By removing this requirement, the modified contract weakens those measures, increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation and potentially undermining a claim for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: All disclosures contemplated hereunder shall be completed within six (6) months from the date of the last party to execute this Agreement. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years from the date of the last disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of five (5) years.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'from the date of the last disclosure hereunder' introduces ambiguity as to when the 5-year period actually begins. This is particularly problematic because under New York law, statutes of limitations for breach of contract claims begin to run from the date of the breach. The original clause made the NDA duration clear and measurable, whereas the altered clause does not.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 213 sets the statute of limitations for breach of contract at six years. Without a clear starting point for the NDA's duration (as in the original text), it becomes more difficult to determine when a breach occurred and when the statute of limitations expires, potentially hindering the Discloser's ability to enforce the NDA within the legally prescribed timeframe. The removal of the last disclosure date makes the trigger for the duration ambiguous, which New York contract law tends to disfavor.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts established legal principles regarding clarity and enforceability, specifically concerning the statute of limitations for breach of contract claims under New York law. By removing 'from the date of the last disclosure hereunder,' the changed text introduces ambiguity about when the 5-year period commences, which is critical for determining when a breach occurred and when the statute of limitations expires, as noted in the Law Explanation. The scraped law snippet 2, NY CPLR § 213, confirms a six-year statute of limitations for contract actions, emphasizing the importance of a clear starting point. The omission directly undermines the enforceability of the NDA.
